coredata.dc:title: Nucleus-targeted carbon dots as peroxidase nanozyme for photoacoustic imaging and phototherapy of tumor
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shanshan
Wang, Jianfeng
Wang, Xuemin
Guo, Yimin
Guan, Shaokang
Zhang, Tao
coredata.dc:description: High-purity carbon dots (CDs) with a highly π-conjugated sp2-hybridized graphite structure were prepared by the pulse electrolysis method using the graphite plate as raw material. Photoacoustic signal together with photothermal effect was found in the CDs-dispersed suspensions under near-infrared (NIR) irradiation. For the suspension with the CDs concentration of 500 μg/mL, the photothermal conversion efficiency is high up 64.3% and the solution’s temperature can be increased to 82.2 °C under NIR irradiation. Moreover, CDs can be effectively endocytosed by human hepatoma (HepG2) cells with a few hours, act as peroxidase nanozyme to decompose H2O2 and facilitate the production of reactive oxygen species. Under NIR irradiation, CDs exhibit an outstanding apoptosis-inducing effect on HepG2 cells by the photothermal effect. In addition, in vivo experiments show that CDs can be used in photoacoustic imaging (PAI) and guiding the tumor treatment. As a result, the nucleus-targeted CDs with an unique combination of PAI and photothermal effect have potential in cancer diagnosis and treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Peroxidase
Nucleus-targeted
Photoacoustic imaging
Photothermal effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo photoacoustic imaging and photothermal imaging
In vivo antitumor activity and biosafety
CDs with good absorption ability of NIR light
Photoacoustic and photothermal images of CDs-dispersed suspensions
Photothermal therapy combined with nucleus-targeted effect of CDs
In vivo image and photothermal therapy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos.: 51971006 and 51571005) and the Special research project on the construction of master's degree programs applied for by Handan University (Grant Nos.: 24).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of preparation of CDs via pulse electrolysis method; (b) high-resolution TEM image, (c) size distribution, (d) XRD pattern, (e) UV–vis absorption spectra and (f) FTIR of CDs; (g) UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of the CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations; (h) XPS full spectrum and (i) high-resolution C 1 s spectra of CDs.

(a) PAI and (b) PA intensity of CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations under laser irradiation; (c) photothermal heating curves of pure water and CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations under 808 nm laser irradiation; (d) photothermal images of CDs-dispersed suspensions under laser irradiation for 0, 1, 3, 5 and 10 min; (e) temperature change of pure water and CDs-dispersed suspensions after 15 min laser irradiation.

(a) Recycling heating profiles of the CDs-dispersed suspension (200 μg/mL) in eppendorf tube; (b, d, f, h and j) photothermal heating and natural cooling of CDs-dispersed suspensions (50, 100, 200, 300 and 500 μg/mL) with one laser on/off photothermal cycle in quartz cuvette; (c, e, g, i and k) plots of time versus -ln(θ) corrpesponding to the natural cooling stage of photothermal heating curve in (b, d, f, h and j), respectively.

(a) Peroxidase activity of CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations (0, 10, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL) using TMB as substrate; (b) the decomposition mechanism of CDs-dispersed suspension (100 μg/mL) using TMB as substrate within different scavengers. Insets in (a) and (b) display the color of reaction solutions; (c) Michaelis-Menten fitting and (d) Lineweaver-Burk fitting of CDs (100 μg/mL).

Cytotoxicity evaluation of CDs-dispersed suspensions with different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 300 and 500 μg/mL) on (a) L-02 and (b) HepG2 cells under 808 nm laser irradiation for 5 min or not; (c) cytotoxicity evaluation of the 200 μg/mL CDs-dispersed suspensions on HepG2 cells under 808 nm laser irradiating different times (0, 3, 5 and 10 min); (d) CLSM images of HepG2 cells treated with CDs-FITC (green), while the nuclei were stained in blue using a Hoechst 33258 staining; (e) cellular uptake and (f) cellular efflux results of HepG2 cells treated with CDs-dispersed suspensions for different time (0, 2, 4, 8, 16 and 24 h); (g) quantitative mean fluorescence intensities corresponding the cellular uptake and efflux in (e) and (f), respectively.

(a) Live/dead staining and (b) quantitative analysis of CDs-induced apoptosis of HepG2 cells under 808 nm laser irradiation for different times (0, 3, 5 and 10 min); (c) apoptosis assay and (d) quantitative analysis of CDs-induced apoptosis of HepG2 cells under 808 nm laser irradiation for different times (0, 3, 5 and 10 min).

(a) Fluorescence images and (b) 3D image of fluorescence intensity of CDs-induced intracellular ROS of HepG2 cells under 808 nm laser irradiating different times (0, 3, 5 and 10 min); (c) fluorescence images and (d) 3D image of fluorescence intensity of CDs-induced mitochondrial membrane potential of HepG2 cells under 808 nm laser irradiating different times (0, 3, 5 and 10 min).

(a) In vivo PAI of mice injected with 0.1 mLCDs-dispersed suspension (2 mg/mL) at different post-injection times and (b) quantitative PA intensity of tumor, the insert in (a) is the tumor photographs; (c) in vivo thermal images of mice injected with CDs-dispersed suspension under laser irradiation for different times; (d) tumor volume, (e) tumor photographs, (f) tumor weight and (g) body weight curves at 9 days post injection; (h) H&E staining of heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney tissues.

head.ce:title: Nucleus-targeted carbon dots as peroxidase nanozyme for photoacoustic imaging and phototherapy of tumor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-purity carbon dots (CDs) with a highly π-conjugated sp2-hybridized graphite structure were prepared by the pulse electrolysis method using the graphite plate as raw material. Photoacoustic signal together with photothermal effect was found in the CDs-dispersed suspensions under near-infrared (NIR) irradiation. For the suspension with the CDs concentration of 500 μg/mL, the photothermal conversion efficiency is high up 64.3% and the solution’s temperature can be increased to 82.2 °C under NIR irradiation. Moreover, CDs can be effectively endocytosed by human hepatoma (HepG2) cells with a few hours, act as peroxidase nanozyme to decompose H2O2 and facilitate the production of reactive oxygen species. Under NIR irradiation, CDs exhibit an outstanding apoptosis-inducing effect on HepG2 cells by the photothermal effect. In addition, in vivo experiments show that CDs can be used in photoacoustic imaging (PAI) and guiding the tumor treatment. As a result, the nucleus-targeted CDs with an unique combination of PAI and photothermal effect have potential in cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Early accurate diagnosis and precise therapy for cancer are essential to improve the prognosis of patients. Compared with conventional imaging, photoacoustic imaging (PAI) has been widely used as an advanced diagnostic tool in the medical field due to its deep tissue penetration, non-invasive control, high resolution, accuracy and sensitivity [1]. On the other hand, photothermal therapy (PTT) has attracted great attention due to its low side effects, minimal invasiveness, and tumor specific localization [2–5]. Biocompatible nanoparticles are of great significance for the diagnosis and treatment of tumor. In particular, carbon dots (CDs) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high specific surface area, small size, large π-conjugated structure, good biocompatibility and tunable fluorescence have great application potential in drug carriers, bioimaging and PTT [6–9].

Current studies of CDs’ imaging mainly focus on fluorescence imaging, however, only a few studies on PAI of CDs were reported [10–14]. Furthermore, although some advances on PTT of CDs for cancer treatment have been achieved [15,16], the photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of most CDs is still relatively low, as summarized in Table S1 (Supplementary material). To help understand and improve the PCE of CDs, two mechanisms have been proposed, including localized surface plasmon resonance effect and electron-hole generation nuclear relaxation and conjugation or hyperconjugation effect [17]. Recently, several CDs with relatively higher PEC have been reported [18,19]. For example, Moniruzzaman et al. [18] prepared Y-CQDs with larger polyaromatic sp2 domain and oxidized surface and found that their PCE is 32.6%. Han et al. [19] synthesized CDs with a very high PCE of about 83.7% by solvothermal method using citric acid and urea as raw materials, and dimethyl sulfoxide as solvent. However, the organic raw materials and solvents used in preparation of CDs are not environmentally friendly, and the yield efficiency of CDs is still relatively low [20]. Therefore, it is necessary to take environmental protection and yield efficiency into consideration for the preparation of CDs with PAI and high PCE.

In this work, CDs were prepared by a simple pulse electrolysis method using graphite plates as electrodes in deionized water. Such a preparation method of CDs is pollution-free and environmentally friendly. The yield efficiency of CDs is nearly 100% due to no sample loss during the preparation process. It is found that the current CDs exhibit a high PCE of 64.3% and good PAI property. Subsequently, in vitro cell experiments revealed that CDs have weak inhibition on human normal liver (L-02) cells, while obvious lethality to human hepatoma (HepG2) cells especially under NIR irradiation. Further, the apoptosis-inducing mechanism was revealed by relating to the activity of peroxidase and production of reactive oxygen species. In addition, in vivo biosafety, PAI and PTT of CDs were investigated.

Graphite plates, methanol (MeOH), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were bought from Zhengzhou Chenyang Experimental Equipment Sales Department. HepG2 and L-02 cells were kindly obtained from Shijiazhuang ANCI Biotechnology CO., Ltd. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), MEM medium, Roswell Park Memorial Institute 1640 (RPMI 1640) medium, non-essential amino acids solution, sodium pyruvate, fetal bovine serum (FBS) solution, penicillin-streptomycin solution, trypsin, cell counting kit-8 (CCK8) and acridine orange/ethidium bromide (AO/EB) were bought from Zhengzhou Runyan Trading CO., Ltd. JC-1 assay and reactive oxygen species (ROS) assay kit were purchased from Hebei ruipate Biotechnology Co., Ltd.

CDs were synthesized via a facile pulse electrolysis. In detail, two graphite plates were inserted in parallel with a spacing of 10 mm in a beaker (100 mL). About 100 mL of deionized water was injected into beaker, and the two graphite plates respectively connected to the positive/negative terminals of the pulse power supply (RST5200, Risetest, China). The pristine CDs-dispersed suspensions were obtained by electrification and electrolysis for a period of time. The above solution was dialyzed against water with a dialysis device (MWCO 3500 Da) for 12 h to remove impure small molecules.

The morphology of CDs was investigated on a field emission transmission electron microscope (FETEM, Talos F200S G2 FEI) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Chemical state of CDs was measured using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Kalpha) with an Al-Ka source (1486.6 eV). Its structure was identified by an X-ray diffraction (XRD, Panalytical Empyrean) using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). UV–visible spectra were recorded by an UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, PerkinElmer Lambda 1050+ UV/VIS/NIR spectrometer) within the range of 200–1100 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were performed on a spectrometer (VERTEX70) within the range of 4000–400 cm−1.

200 μL of CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 300 and 500 μg/mL) were added to the agarose tubes, respectively. The above agarose tubes were scanned by the PAI instrument (Vevo LAZR-X) with the laser scanning in the wavelength range of 680–970 nm.

The 808 nm laser with a power density of 2.0 W/cm2 and laser power of 2.0 W was used to evaluate the photothermal effect of CDs. Typically, 800 μL of CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 300 and 500 μg/mL) were added to 1.5 mL eppendorf tubes. The above solution was irradiated with 808 nm laser at room temperature. The temperature change of CDs-dispersed suspensions was measured with a digital thermometer. To measure the PCE value (η), CDs-dispersed suspensions were added to a quartz cuvette and exposed to 808 nm laser for 10 min. A digital thermometer was used to register the temperature of the sample during laser irradiation and natural cooling. The η value was defined previously as [21]:
(1)
η = [hS(T max﹣T surr) ﹣ hS(T water·max﹣T surr)]/P(1﹣10-A
808),
where, h and S represent the heat transfer coefficient and surface area of sample container, respectively; T surr, T max and T water·max the room temperature, the highest temperature of sample and water, respectively; P and A
808 the power density of 808 nm laser and the absorbance of sample at 808 nm. The hS value can be calculated using the following formula:
(2)
hS = (m
0 c0+m
1 c2)/τ,
where, m 0 and m 1 are the mass of CDs-dispersed suspensions and quartz cuvette, respectively; c 0 and c 1 the heat capacities of water solution and quartz cuvette, respectively; τ the constant of characteristic thermal time, which is written as:
(3)
τ = t/(-ln(θ)),
where, t is cooling time of CDs-dispersed suspensions and θ is defined as:
(4)
θ = (T-T surr)/(T max -T surr),
where, T is real-time temperature of CDs-dispersed suspensions.

The 50 μL TMB staining solution (0.6 mM) and different concentrations of H2O2 (0, 50, 100, 3200, 500 and 1000 μM) were added to CDs-dispersed suspensions (0, 10, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL). After 30 min, the above solution was transferred to a 96-well cell-culture plate. The absorbance at 630 nm was read on a microplate reader.

Decomposition mechanism of H2O2 was investigated. The 50 μL TMB staining solution (0.6 mM) and H2O2 (500 μM) were added to the CDs-dispersed suspension (100 μg/mL). EDTA (10 mM) and MeOH (10 mM) was added to above solutions, respectively. After 30 min, the above solutions were transferred to a 96-well cell-culture plate. Their absorbance at 630 nm was read on a microplate reader. To study the enzyme kinetics, the absorbance was detected at 1 min intervals, and Michaelis-Menton constant was determined based on the Lineweaver Burk plot (1/VKM/Vmax×1/CH2O2+1/Vmax, where V and Vmax represent the initial reaction rate and the maximum reaction rate, CH2O2 represents the concentration of H2O2, and KM represents the Michaelis Menten constant).

HepG2 cells were cultured in 25 cm2 flasks with MEM medium containing 10% FBS solution, 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution and 1% sodium pyruvate (0.11 g/L) at 37 ℃ in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. At equal environmental conditions, L-02 cells were cultured in 25 cm2 flasks with RPMI 1640 medium containing 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution and 10% FBS solution.

In vitro cytotoxicity and cell proliferation experiment were evaluated by a standard CCK8 assay. HepG2 and L-02 cells were seeded into 24-well cell-culture plate at 3×104/well and 4×104/well for 24 h, respectively. Then, the cells were cultured with the CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 300 and 500 μg/mL) for 4 h. Next, the above cells were irradiated with 808 nm laser for 5 min and further cultured for 4 h. After that, 500 μL of MEM medium and CCK8 kit mixture solution at a ratio of 10:1 was added to each well. After incubated for 3 h, the above solution was equally transferred to three wells in 96-well cell-culture plate. Their absorbance at 450 nm was read on a microplate reader.

CDs were labeled with fluorescein Isothiocyanate (FITC) and represented green fluorescence. FITC-labeled CDs were incubated with HepG2 cells for 3 h, 8 h and 24 h, respectively. HepG2 cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde over 30 min. After stained with Hoechst 33258 solution about 10 min, the above fixed cells were washed with PBS buffer for twice. The confocal laser microcope (Nikon Ti-S) was used to observe the intracellular localization of CDs.

To evaluate the cellular uptake, HepG2 cells were plated in 12-well cell-culture plate at 5×104/well about 24 h and were cultured with FITC-labeled CDs (20 μg/mL) for different time. Final, flow cytometer (BD FACSympony A5) was used to detect the cellular fluorescence intensity of FITC-labeled CDs.

For cellular efflux evaluation, HepG2 cells were cultured with FITC-labeled CDs (20 μg/mL) about 24 h and washed with PBS buffer for twice. The above cells were incubated with fresh MEM medium for different time. The fluorescence intensity of remaining FITC-labeled CDs in cells was measured by a flow cytometer.

HepG2 cells were seeded into 24-well cell-culture plate at 3×104/well for 24 h, and then incubated with 200 μg/mL of CDs for 4 h. The incubated cells were irradiated with 808 nm laser for 0, 3, 5 and 10 min, respectively, and then further cultured for 4 h. The above cells were collected and detected using an Annexin V-FITC/PI detection kit on a flow cytometer. The above cells treated with CDs and NIR irradiation were stained with AO/EB staining solution about 8 min. The stained cells were observed on a fluorescence microscope (Nikon-Eclipse-Ti).

HepG2 cells were treated with 200 μg/mL of CDs for 4 h, and then irradiated with 808 nm laser for 0, 3, 5 and 10 min, respectively. The irradiated cells were cultured for 4 h and stained with 10 μM DCFH-DA (0.5 mL) for 35 min. The above stained cells were washed with PBS buffer for twice and measured by fluorescence microscope.

HepG2 cells were treated with 200 μg/mL of CDs for 4 h. After irradiated with 808 nm laser for 0, 3, 5 and 10 min, respectively, the cells were further cultured for 4 h and then stained with 10 μg/mL JC-1 (0.5 mL) for 25 min. The above stained cells were washed with PBS buffer for twice and observed by fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo photoacoustic imaging and photothermal imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Balb/c-nude mice (male, 4–6 weeks, 18–23 g) were purchased from the Experimental Animal Center in Zhengzhou University. The test mice were given food, water and lived in a 12 h light and 12 h dark cycle environment. HepG2 cells were injected subcutaneously into its right back region of mice. When the tumor size reached to about 60–100 mm3, the above mice were divided at random into two groups (without CDs and within CDs) and were injected with 0.1 mL PBS and CDs-dispersed suspension (2 mg/mL), respectively. The PAI of CDs were detected by the PAI instrument with an excitation range of 680–970 nm at the indicated time points of 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 24 h. At 12 h after injection, the photothermal imaging of CDs were imaged by infrared (IR) camera (HIK VISION) under the NIR irradiation for 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antitumor activity and biosafety
ce:section.ce:para:
Further, in vivo antitumor efficacy and biological safety were determined. When tumor size reached to about 60–100 mm3, the test mice were divided at random into two groups (without CDs and within CDs) and were injected with 0.1 mL PBS and CDs-dispersed suspension (2 mg/mL), respectively. At 12 h after injection, the test mice were irradiated with 808 nm laser (power density of 2.0 W/cm2 and laser power of 2.0 W) for 5 min. The body weight and tumor volume of mice were measured every 3 days. The tumor volume was calculated based on the following formula:
(5) V = a × b 2 × π/6,
where, a and b measured using a vernier caliper represent the longest diameter and the minor diameter of the tumor, respectively. At 6 d after injection, the test mice were irradiated with 808 nm laser for 5 min. At 9 d after injection, blood obtained from the test mice was collected for analyzing the routine haematological parameters. The tumors and main organs including heart, lung, spleen, liver, and kidneys were weighed separately and calculated the relative organ weight (%). Furthermore, the tissue sections were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde solution and stained by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). All the animal experiments were conducted according to ARRIVE guidelines and were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee and Zhengzhou University.

All data in this study were demonstrated as mean ± standard deviations (SD). All experiments were operated three or more times. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was employed to examine the statistical significance by Tukey’s test, and the probability-value (p) less than 0.05 was regarded to be statistically significant (****p < 0.0001, ***p < 0.001, **p < 0.01, and *p < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs with good absorption ability of NIR light
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were prepared by pulse electrolysis method with the graphite plate as a carbon source as shown in Fig. 1(a), and the pristine CDs were purified using a dialysis bag. The facile operability of this strategy is conducive to the practical application. The morphology and dispersion sate of CDs are shown in Fig. 1(b) and (c). It is seen that CDs exhibit a spherical shape and are finely dispersed with a size range of 2–8 nm. The lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.212 nm can be found in the inset of Fig. 1(b), which matches with the (100) plane of grapheme [22]. From the XRD result in Fig. 1(d), the broad diffraction peak at about 2θ = 26.6° corresponding to the (002) lattice fringes of graphite is observable, indicating the graphite structure of CDs [23]. Fig. 1(e) shows the UV–visible spectra of CDs. It is found that CDs exhibit a wide range of light absorption, and have an absorbance peak at about 230 nm which can be attributed to the π-π transition absorption of CC [24]. FTIR spectroscopy was used to confirm the chemical structure of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1(f), the absorption bands at about 3341 cm−1 and 1713 cm−1 in FTIR of CDs correspond to the O-H and CO, respectively. The absorption bands at 1451 cm−1 and 1236 cm−1 correspond to the CC and C-O, respectively. The result is well consistent with the finding of previous studies [25,26]. Further, the absorption property of the CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations was measured by UV–vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 1(g), the suspensions of CDs exhibit a broad and strong absorption from UV to NIR region. The absorption intensity becomes stronger with increasing the concentration of CDs. It is generally believed that the strong absorption in the NIR region facilitates the effective NIR photothermal conversion. The high NIR absorption of CDs may be related to their chemical structure. From the XPS spectrum in Fig. 1(h), it can be seen that CDs exhibit a dominant graphitic C1s peak at 282.3 eV (83.60%) and O1s peak at 530 eV (16.40%). The enlarged spectrum of C1s in Fig. 1(i) displays a major sp2 carbon (CC) peak at 282.2 eV (51.53%), C-O peak at 283.6 eV (38.24%), and CO peak at 286.6 eV (10.23%). This indicates that the carbon atoms are dominated by sp2 hybridization. Combined with the above analysis of UV–vis absorption spectra and FTIR spectra, it is concluded that the prepared CDs have an obvious CC structure. Meanwhile, the results also support the fact that sp2 in CDs forms a highly delocalized large π bond, which can achieve the NIR absorption. Fig. S1 (Supplementary material) shows the stability of CDs in deionized water, PBS, and MEM culture medium. The zeta potentials of CDs in deionized water, PBS, and MEM culture medium is −39.8 mV, −32.0 mV, and −18.4 mV, respectively, demonstrating that the stability of CDs in MEM culture medium is slightly lower than that in PBS and deionized water. However, CDs are still considered to have good dispersibility in deionized water, PBS, and MEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoacoustic and photothermal images of CDs-dispersed suspensions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2(a) and (b) show the PAI and PA intensity of CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations. Clearly, CDs-dispersed suspensions exhibit an obvious PA signal. Moreover, their PA intensity increases with an increase in the concentration CDs. This is mainly related to the higher temperature of CDs dispersed suspension. CDs dispersion suspension under NIR irradiation has a higher temperature, resulting in thermal expansion and pressure wave, namely the detected PA signal [27]. Therefore, the higher the CDs temperature rises, the stronger the PA signal intensity. The curves of temperature versus time for the CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations under 808 nm laser irradiation are plotted in Fig. 2(c). For comparison, the data of pure water is also shown in Fig. 2(c). With increasing the laser irradiation time from 0 to 15 min, the temperature of pure water increases slightly from 29.1 to 33.1 °C. In sharp contrast, the temperature of suspensions increases significantly especially in the initial stage of laser irradiation. In addition, the variation tendency becomes more significant with increasing the concentration of CDs. Fig. 2(d) shows the infrared thermal images of suspensions under laser irradiation for different times. The temperature rising of suspensions with irradiation time can be observed intuitively, which suggests that CDs can efficiently convert the absorbed near-infrared light into thermal energy. The temperature variation of the CDs-dispersed suspensions after 15 min irradiation is shown Fig. 2(e). For the suspension with a CDs concentration of 500 μg/mL, the temperature increases by about 54.3 °C. The higher photothermal effect is related to the high NIR absorption of CDs. As a result, it is concluded that the current CDs have a strong PA signal and excellent in vitro photothermal effect.

Further, the photothermal stability of the CDs-dispersed suspensions was evaluated in five laser on/off cycles at a power density of 2 W/cm2. As an example, the recycling heating profile of the 200 μg/mL CDs-dispersed suspension in eppendorf tube is shown in Fig. 3(a). No noticeable degradation of the maximum temperature was detected within five laser on-off cycles, suggesting that CDs possess excellent photothermal stability. From Fig. 2(a), it is observed that all the photothermal heating curves exhibit an obvious plateau, suggesting that the temperature change of suspensions becomes insignificant after 10 min NIR irradiation. Thereby, 10 min of irradiation time was selected for the photostability test. The PCE value of the CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations was evaluated using one laser on/off photothermal cycle as shown in Fig. 3(b). From Fig. 3(b), T and T max can be obtained and θ values were calculated using Equ. (4). By plotting the data of t versus -ln(θ) and linear fitting the data, τ values were estimated from the slope of fitting lines. When τ value was substituted into Equ. (2), we can get the values of hS. Finally, hS values were substituted into Equ. (1), the PCE values of the CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentration of 50, 100, 200, 300 and 500 μg/mL were calculated as 41.9%, 51.4%, 51.7%, 57.6% and 64.3% Fig. 3(b-k), respectively. Obviously, the PCE value of the CDs-dispersed suspensions is concentration-dependent. As listed in Table S2, the PCE values of the current materials are much greater than that reported for most of other photothermal conversion nanomaterials, i.e., gold nanoshell [28], hollow carbon nanospheres [29], polydopamine [30], metal-organic framework derived carbon nanospheres [31], boron quantum dots [32] and carbon dots prepared by other methods [33]. The high PCE value is helpful for biomaterials to enhance the efficiency of photothermal therapy.

It is well known that the level of H2O2 in tumor cells is high, and its decomposition will produce ROS which can kill cells. Herein, TMB staining method was used to detect the peroxidase activity of CDs. As shown in Fig. S2 (Supplementary material), with an increase in the concentration of H2O2, the color of TMB gradually turns blue after 30 min decomposition, indicating that the number of ROS increases. In addition, the concentration effect of CDs on the peroxidase activity is shown in Fig. 4(a). With increasing the concentration of CDs, the color of reaction solution deepened gradually (inset), accompanied by the increase in the amount of ROS. It has reported that the decomposition mechanism of H2O2 mainly includes hydroxyl radical mechanism and electron transfer mechanism. In order to illustrate the decomposition mechanism by CDs, MeOH and EDTA were used as hydroxyl radical and hole scavengers to be studied, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4(b), compared with the control group, the absorbance of MeOH group decreases slightly after 30 min decomposition, while that of EDTA group decreases significantly. This result suggests that the decomposition of H2O2 by CDs is mainly dominated by the electron hole mechanism. Fig. 4(c) and (d) show the affinity and enzyme-mimicking activity of CDs calculated based on Michaelis–Menten steady-state kinetics. According to the Lineweaver Burk fitting equation (Y = 0.29 × 10−8 + 1.02 × 10−8), the Michaelis-Menten constant (KM) and its maximum velocity (Vmax) of CDs are 0.28 mM and 9.8 × 10−9 M·s−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal therapy combined with nucleus-targeted effect of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Biosafety of nanomaterials is a precondition for their biological applications [4]. The cytotoxicity of CDs for photothermal therapy was evaluated using standard CCK8 methods. As shown in Fig. 5(a), compared with the control group, the L-02 cells treated with CDs-dispersed suspensions at different concentrations do not decrease significantly without NIR irradiation, and the cell viability for all the sample groups is above 90%. Under NIR irradiation, CDs still have no obvious toxicity on L-02 cells when the concentration of CDs is lower than 200 μg/mL. Moreover, as can be seen from Fig. S3 (Supplementary material), it appears that the cytotoxicity of CDs at a relatively low dose (200 μg/mL) on L-02 cells is irradiation time-independent, which is mainly due to the fact that the temperature of CDs in the culture medium under laser irradiation is lower than that in water (Fig. S4). This is mainly due to the aggregation of CDs in the culture medium after light irradiation. However, once the concentration of CDs is higher than 200 μg/mL, the cell viability would decrease significantly. From Fig. 5(b), it can also be observed that, without NIR irradiation, low concentrations of CDs have no obvious toxicity and side effects on HepG2 cells, while high concentrations of CDs (300 and 500 μg/mL) have a certain inhibitory effect on HepG2 cells. In contrast, under NIR irradiation, the CDs-dispersed suspensions exhibit a significant inhibitory effect on HepG2 cells and the inhibitory effect increases with an increase in the concentration of CDs, as shown in Fig. 5(c). It is confirmed that the photothermal effect of CDs has an effective cancer-killing ability. Moreover, the cell viability will continue to decline to about 41.9% with increasing the light irradiation time to 10 min, suggesting that prolonging NIR irradiating time is favorable to enhance the PTT effect. These results reveal that CDs exhibit obvious toxicity on HepG2 cells but low cytotoxicity on L-02 cells under NIR irradiation when the concentration of CDs is below 300 μg/mL. To further investigate the nucleus-targeted effect of CDs, the prepared CDs were labeled with FITC green fluorescent agent, the nuclei were stained with the blue fluorescent staining Hoechst 33258, and the intracellular distribution of CDs was observed by laser confocal microscopy. As shown in Fig. 5(d), after co-cultured with HepG2 cells for 3 h, most of CDs are distributed in the cytoplasm except a very small amount in the nucleus. With increasing the co-culture time to 8 h, the amount of CDs in the nucleus increases. As the culture time further increases to 24 h, almost all the CDs are distributed in the nucleus. This result indicates that CDs can enter the nucleus in a short time to achieve nuclear targeting, which has great implication for the precision cancer treatment. As well known, the common targeted therapies include mitochondrial targeting [6], nuclear targeting [22] and lysosomal targeting [34]. Among them, nuclear targeting is one of the most effective treatment strategies owing to the nucleus contains most of genetic material. Thus, the nucleus-targeted strategy can be achieved to directly disrupt and interfere with the genetic information from the level of the nucleus, the therapeutic effect will be improved effectively. From the merged image in the Fig. 5(d), green fluorescence of FITC-CDs well overlaps with blue fluorescence of Hoechst-33258. The Pearson correlation coefficient between CDs and Hoechst 33258 is about 0.78. Fig. 5(e)-(g) show the cellular uptake and cellular efflux results. Clearly most CDs can be internalized into cells within 8 h, and only a few amount of CDs efflux out of cells after 24 h of internalization, indicating that strong capacity of CDs to enhance the cellular internalization as well as decrease the cellular efflux.

The photothermal effect combined with targeting effect of the current CDs shows that CDs have a strong therapeutic effect on tumors. Hence, the therapeutic efficiency of CDs on HepG2 and L-02 cells was further detected by fluorescence microscope using AO (green, living cells) and EB (red, death cells) double-staining method in Fig. 6(a) and (b). The number of the red-fluorescence of dead cells is the lowest in the control group and is the highest in the CDs group under 10 min NIR irradiation. There are no many red-fluorescent death cells when cells were treated with CDs without NIR irradiation. The number of the death cells increases gradually with increasing the NIR irradiation time. However, even if the NIR irradiation time increases, no obvious dead cells can be observed for the L-02 cells in Fig. S5 (Supplementary material). It is suggested that CDs have good biocompatibility on L-02 cells, while can efficiently induce apoptosis of HepG2 cells under NIR irradiation. Furthermore, the cell apoptosis of HepG2 cells treated with CDs under NIR irradiation was analyzed on a flow cytometer. As shown in Fig. 6(c) and (d), compared with the control group, the CDs groups exhibit a higher apoptosis ratio. Especially for the CDs group under laser irradiation for 10 min, the apoptosis ratio is increased to 68.0%. This result further demonstrates that CDs have an outstanding apoptosis-inducing effect on HepG2 cells under NIR irradiation.

To verify the mitochondrial-mediated pathways and ROS oxidative damage of induced apoptosis, the intracellular ROS level was measured using DCF probe and the mitochondrial membrane potential was detected by JC-1 assay. As shown in Fig. 7(a) and (b), the green fluorescence intensity of HepG2 cells in the CDs groups is stronger than that in the control group, and the fluorescence intensity in the CDs sample groups gradually increases under NIR irradiation. According to the analysis of peroxidase activity by CDs, the high content of H2O2 in tumor cells and its decomposition induced by CDs is responsible for the production of cellular ROS. In contrast, the green fluorescence intensity of L-02 cells has no significant change between the control group and the sample group (Fig. S6). indicating that CDs have little effect on the cellular ROS level in L-02 cells. The results of cell mitochondrial membrane potential measured are shown in Fig. 7(c) and (d). The red fluorescence (JC-1 aggregates) and green fluorescence (JC-1 monomers) represent the normal and damaged mitochondrial membranes, respectively. It is seen that only red fluorescence was observed in the control group, indicating that cells have a complete mitochondrial membrane. In comparison, the green fluorescence appears in the CDs sample group of HepG2 cells, and the green and red fluorescence intensities increase and decrease gradually with increasing the NIR irradiation time. When the irradiation time increases to 10 min, almost all the cells exhibit green fluorescence, indicating that CDs would severely damage the mitochondrial membrane under NIR irradiation. However, no obvious different was observed, and all groups exhibit red fluorescence in L-02 cells (Fig. S7). These results provide a clear evidence that CDs can increase the ROS level of HepG2 cells, decrease the mitochondrial membrane potential, and thus induce apoptosis of HepG2 cells. However, CDs have no apoptosis-inducing effect on L-02 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo image and photothermal therapy
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of in vitro data, the in vivo imaging and therapeutic effect of CDs were further studied. As a novel non-destructive biomedical imaging method, PAI can produce the high-resolution and high-contrast biological tissue images. Fig. 8(a) and (b) show the in vivo PA signal of CDs in tumor. Unlike the PBS group, an obvious PA signal can be observed at the tumor site in the CDs group. Clearly, the intensity of PA signal gradually increases with the increase of post-injection time, reaching a maximum value at 6 h, then gradually decreases. The result indicates that the PAI of CDs achieves the diagnostic function. According to the infrared thermal mapping in Fig. 8(c), the temperature change of tumor in the CDs group is more significant compared with that observed in the control group. Specially, the temperature of tumor is increased to 60.1 ℃ when the laser irradiation is up to 5 min, which would cause irreversible damage to the tumor tissue [35–37]. The PTT effect of CDs was also evaluated by measuring the variation of tumor volume and weight. As shown in Fig. 8(d), the tumor volume in the CDs group increases more slowly than that in the PBS group. At 9-days post-injection time, the mean size and weight of tumor in the CDs group are smaller than those in the PBS group, as can be seen from Fig. 8(e) and (f). In addition, the in vivo biosafety of CDs was also studied by comparing the weight change of mice, relative weight (%) of organs and H&E staining of visceral tissues. The change of body weight in the CDs group at different post-injection time point exhibits no obvious abnormality compared with that in the PBS group (Fig. 8(g)). The relative weight (%) of organs shown in Table S3 (Supplementary material) has also no obvious difference between CDs and control groups. Furthermore, form the H&E staining of visceral tissues in Fig. 8(h), no obvious tissue damage was observed in the CDs group, indicating that the current CDs have good in vivo biosafety.

In this paper, CDs were prepared by pulsed electrolysis. The prepared CDs exhibit an ultra-high yielding efficiency and high photothermal conversion ability with PCE value of about 64.3%, and excellent PAI. In vitro experiments show that CDs can significantly inhibit the cell proliferation and induce apoptosis under NIR laser irradiation. CDs can be targeted into the nucleus within a few hours, which is good great promise to achieve potential cancer therapeutic effect. The cellular uptake and efflux evaluations show that CDs can be efficiently endocytosed into cells and at the same time reduce the cellular efflux. CDs can decompose H2O2, resulting in the increase of ROS in HepG2 cells, and have little effect on L-02 cells. As a result, CDs induce effectively the mitochondrial dysfunction on HepG2 cells under 808 nm laser irradiation, resulting in the loss of mitochondrial membrane potential due to the photothermal effect. In vivo experiments show that CDs can achieve PAI as a diagnostic tools and PTT effect as a therapeutic tool. The novel of nuclear targeted CDs that integrate diagnosis and treatment have great application prospects in cancer treatment.

Shaokang Guan: Resources. Tao Zhang: Resources, Conceptualization. Xuemin Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yimin Guo: Formal analysis. Shanshan Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Jianfeng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos.: 51971006 and 51571005) and the Special research project on the construction of master's degree programs applied for by Handan University (Grant Nos.: 24).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.113950.
